data.title: Indonesia’s sovereign wealth fund considers involvement in $7 billion Grab-GoTo deal - report
data.description:
Newly launched sovereign wealth fund Danantara Indonesia is in early talks with GoTo (OTCPK:GTOFF) to get a piece of U.S.-listed rival Grab's (NASDAQ:GRAB) potential buyout of the ride-hailing and food delivery firm, Bloomberg reported on Friday.

The fund is seeking a minority stake in the combined entity, which could help alleviate the Indonesian government's concerns about Singapore-headquartered Grab (NASDAQ:GRAB) owning the country's largest tech firm, the report said.

Last month, Indonesia's antitrust regulator launched a study to assess potential risks arising from a possible merger between the tech giants, although the companies have not yet confirmed any merger discussions.

The companies have made progress on the deal's structure, but talks have slowed down recently due to potential regulatory demands, Bloomberg News reported.

Grab, backed by Uber Technologies (UBER), is looking to strike a deal in the second quarter and could value GoTo at around $7 billion, sources with knowledge of the matter told Reuters last month.

In February, Indonesia launched Danantara, an investment entity designed to fund a broad spectrum of projects, ranging from metal processing to artificial intelligence. It will manage government stakes in state-owned companies and is modeled after Singapore’s investment arm, Temasek.

data.title: Asia markets mixed post-Trump-Xi call; U.S. futures steady ahead of May jobs report
data.description:
Asia stock markets trade mixed on Friday, tracking a downbeat session on Wall Street overnight, weighed down by a dispute between Trump and Elon Musk, and renewed US-China trade uncertainty.

Gold rose to around $3,370 per ounce on Friday, climbing back to a four-week-high.

Japan (NKY:IND) rose 0.37% rose 0.4% to above 37,600 while the broader Topix Index gained 0.4% to 2,767 on Friday, reversing losses from the previous session. The Japanese yen slipped toward 144 per dollar on Friday, extending losses from the previous session.

On the domestic front, data showed that Japan’s household spending unexpectedly declined in April, as rising prices continued to weigh on consumer demand. 

Japan’s foreign reserves fell slightly by $110 million to $1.298 trillion in May 2025, remaining close to the highest level since mid-2022.

China (SHCOMP) fell 0.06% traded around the flat line on Friday, struggling for clear direction as investors assessed the phone call between US President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping, and the offshore yuan weakened toward 7.18 per dollar on Friday, extending losses from the previous session.

President Trump and China’s Xi Jinping spoke on Thursday and agreed that officials from both sides would soon resume negotiations aimed at ending the trade war, though no concrete details were provided. 

Investors now turn their attention to upcoming Chinese inflation and trade data, due Monday, for fresh signals on the health of the world’s second-largest economy. 

Hong Kong (HSI) fell 0.09% to 23,834 in early trade on Friday, halting gains from the previous session.

India (SENSEX) rose 0.18% The Reserve Bank of India lowered its key repo rate by 50 basis points to 5.50% at its May meeting, marking the third consecutive monthly rate cut.

Australia (AS51) fell 0.14% nearly flat to around 8,540 in early trading Friday, but on track for a fourth weekly gain. The Australian dollar slipped toward $0.650 on Friday, ending a two-day winning streak, amid cautious market sentiment.

Private house approvals in Australia increased by 3.1% month-over-month to a seven-month high of 9,349 units in April 2025.

The seasonally adjusted number of total dwellings approved in Australia fell 5.7% month-over-month to an eight-month low of 14,633 units in April 2025.

In the U.S., on Thursday, all three major indexes ended lower as investors reacted to a public disagreement between President Trump and Elon Musk, renewed U.S.-China trade uncertainty, and increasing signs of a weakening labor market.

U.S. stock futures stabilized on Friday as investors awaited the closely watched May jobs report, expected to shed light on the state of the labor market: Dow +0.32%; S&P 500 +0.29%; Nasdaq +0.23%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

More on Asia:

Australia's trade surplus narrows in April to AUD 5.41B on weaker exports, higher imports

China's services sector growth accelerates in May amid stronger business & activity

Australia's Q1 GDP misses estimates amid weak public spending, consumer demand, and exports

Australia's manufacturing stalls at -23.5 in May, services sector continues 16-month growth

Japan services PMI revised upward to 51.0 in May 2025

data.title: Friday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
8:30 AM Employment Situation

Nonfarm payrolls track the number of part-time and full-time employees in both business and government. Economists forecast 129K jobs to added during the May month, and for the unemployment rate to hold steady at 4.2%.

1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count

The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs. The active rig count acts as a leading indicator of demand for oil products. The active rig count acts as a leading indicator of demand for oil products. Crude Oil Rigs in the United States fell to 461 in May 30 week from 465 in the previous week.

3:00 PM Consumer Credit  Changes in consumer credit indicate the state of consumer finances and portend future spending patterns. The report includes credit cards, vehicle loans, and student loans; mortgages are not included. 

An identical, trend-like increase of $10.2 billion expected in April after a gain of $10.2 billion in March.

data.title: Samsara anticipates 24% revenue growth for FY26 while expanding AI-powered safety and OEM partnerships
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Samsara Inc. (IOT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Sanjit Biswas reported "Samsara delivered a strong Q1 of our new fiscal year, surpassing $1.5 billion in ARR" and highlighted that ARR reached $1.54 billion, growing 31% year-over-year adjusted for constant currency. He noted growth in customers with more than $100,000 in ARR by 154, an increase of 35% year-over-year.
  • Biswas emphasized, "We're doubling down on safety and recently launched new positive recognition tools within the Samsara platform," including streaks, milestones, and personalized kudos, using gamification to enhance employee engagement and safety outcomes.
  • The company expanded major partnerships with OEMs: "We're partnering with Hyundai Translead, Stellantis, and Rivian" to enable cloud-to-cloud connections, streamline electric fleet management, and integrate video-based safety features.
  • CFO Dominic Phillips stated, "Q1 FY '26 was highlighted by strong top line growth and continued efficiency gains." He discussed a "multimillion dollar impact" from elongated sales cycles after tariff-related events but noted a record pipeline and generally consistent win rates.

Outlook

  • For Q2, management expects revenue between $371 million and $373 million, representing 24% year-over-year growth, and non-GAAP operating margin of 9%. Non-GAAP EPS is expected between $0.06 and $0.07.
  • For full year FY '26, revenue is projected between $1.547 billion and $1.555 billion, with year-over-year growth of 24% to 25% adjusted for constant currency, non-GAAP operating margin of approximately 13%, and non-GAAP EPS between $0.39 and $0.41.
  • Phillips explained, "Our guidance philosophy is similar to previous quarters. We're trying to make sure that we're setting guidance with a lot of confidence and that accounts for various downside scenarios."

Financial Results

  • Ending ARR for Q1 was $1.54 billion, up 31% year-over-year. Q1 revenue reached $367 million, also up 31% year-over-year or 32% on a constant currency basis.
  • The company ended Q1 with 2,638 customers generating $100,000-plus ARR, a 35% year-over-year increase. ARR per $100,000-plus customer increased to $338,000, and this segment comprised 58% of total ARR, up from 56% a year earlier.
  • Non-GAAP gross margin set a quarterly record at 79%. Non-GAAP operating margin was 14%, up from 2% in the year-ago quarter. Adjusted free cash flow margin was 12%, compared to 7% last year.
  • International business contributed 18% of net new ACV, tied for the highest quarterly contribution, with Europe delivering its highest quarterly net new ACV mix.

Q&A

  • Aleksandr J. Zukin, Wolfe: Asked about sales cycle elongation and pipeline quality. Phillips replied, "The construction of the deals didn't change. We ended up closing a number of them in May...it was a multimillion dollar impact. The pipeline was good. We had a record pipeline generation quarter in Q1."
  • Zukin, Wolfe: Inquired about OEM partnership impact. Biswas responded, "We're just trying to make it as easy as possible for our customers to get their operations into the Samsara Connected Operations Cloud...We think that will help us get deeper insights into the performance of these assets and...reduce friction."
  • Keith Weiss, Morgan Stanley: Asked about differentiating macro-related deal elongation from bigger deal complexity. Phillips noted, "Liberation Day hits, higher tariffs come on board...customers saying, 'Hey, I'm buying a lot of tariff-impacted vehicles...' that can just delay conversations."
  • Weiss, Morgan Stanley: Questioned vertical impact. Phillips stated, "I think it was widespread. I think across all of our end markets...a lot of those goods are impacted by tariffs."
  • Michael James Turrin, Wells Fargo: Asked about construction and public sector verticals. Biswas said, "It's really being driven by a desire to increase efficiency...these are areas that haven't digitized as heavily as you might expect."
  • Matt Hedberg, RBC: Inquired about asset tags and ACV contribution. Phillips explained, "It depends on a given deal...customers are trying it out on one subset of assets before rolling it out wall-to-wall."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused repeatedly on macro uncertainty, sales cycles, and the impact of tariffs, with a slightly cautious tone seeking clarity on deal timing and margin sustainability.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, repeatedly citing strong pipeline, customer demand, and ROI. Phillips stated, "We feel good about the pipeline...big market growing quickly, big opportunity for digital transformation."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained confident, but the Q&A saw greater emphasis on macroeconomic risks and timing uncertainties, prompted by analyst queries.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for full-year FY '26 revenue increased from a prior range of $1.523 billion to $1.533 billion up to $1.547 billion to $1.555 billion, reflecting heightened confidence despite macro challenges.
  • The pace of large customer growth remained robust, though a methodology change adjusted how customer counts are measured. Gross margin improved to 79% from 78% in Q4, while operating margin declined from 16% to 14%, as Q4 typically realizes higher margin leverage.
  • Management’s narrative shifted to highlight OEM integration and AI-powered maintenance, while analyst focus shifted more toward macro-related sales cycle risks and vertical-specific adoption.

Risks and Concerns

  • Phillips addressed "instances of elongated sales cycles...as some customers prioritize spending on tariff-impacted goods such as vehicles, equipment and other assets." He noted ongoing macro uncertainty and timing risk for deal closures.
  • Management noted complexity in maintenance at scale, the impact of tariffs, and supply chain resilience as current customer concerns, with AI solutions positioned as mitigation.
  • Analysts questioned the potential for further margin expansion, the impact of OEM partnerships on margins, and the pace of adoption in international and public sector markets.

Final Takeaway

Samsara launched fiscal 2026 with strong ARR and revenue growth, expanded major OEM partnerships, and introduced new AI-driven safety and maintenance features. Management reaffirmed confidence in durable, efficient growth, raising full-year guidance while acknowledging elongated sales cycles due to macro and tariff-related uncertainty. The company emphasized continued investments in AI, product expansion, and international growth as key drivers for the year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Broadcom forecasts $5.1B AI semiconductor revenue for Q3 2025 as AI networking demand accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Hock E. Tan, President and CEO, opened the call by highlighting "total revenue was a record $15 billion, up 20% year-on-year. This 20% year-on-year growth was all organic as Q2 last year was the first full quarter with VMware." Tan attributed the strong performance to "continued strength in AI semiconductors and the momentum we have achieved in VMware."
  • Semiconductor revenue reached $8.4 billion with growth accelerating to 17% year-on-year, driven by AI semiconductor revenue of over $4.4 billion, which was up 46% year-on-year. Tan stated, "AI networking, which is based on Ethernet was robust and represented 40% of our AI revenue."
  • The company announced the Tomahawk 6 switch, which Tan described as "the next-generation 102.4 terabits per second switch capacity" and noted its significance in enabling "clusters of more than 100,000 AI accelerators to be deployed in just 2 tiers instead of 3."
  • Tan indicated that "we may actually see an acceleration of XPU demand into the back half of 2026 to meet urgent demand for inference on top of the demand we have indicated from training."
  • For infrastructure software, Q2 revenue reached $6.6 billion, up 25% year-on-year, and Tan reported "over 87%" of the 10,000 largest customers have now adopted VCF.
  • Kirsten M. Spears, CFO, stated, "Consolidated revenue was a record $15 billion for the quarter, up 20% from a year ago. Gross margin was 79.4% of revenue in the quarter, better than we originally guided on product mix."

Outlook

  • The company guided Q3 consolidated revenue to be approximately $15.8 billion, up 21% year-on-year. Spears said, "We forecast semiconductor revenue of approximately $9.1 billion, up 25% year-on-year. Within this, we expect Q3 AI semiconductor revenue of $5.1 billion, up 60% year-on-year."
  • Infrastructure software revenue is expected to be approximately $6.7 billion, up 16% year-on-year. Spears added, "We expect Q3 adjusted EBITDA to be at least 66%."
  • Management indicated, "we anticipate now our fiscal 2025 growth rate of AI semiconductor revenue to sustain into fiscal 2026."

Financial Results

  • Q2 consolidated adjusted EBITDA reached $10 billion, up 35% year-on-year, representing 67% of revenue.
  • Semiconductor solutions segment revenue was $8.4 billion, with gross margin approximately 69% and operating margin of 57%.
  • Infrastructure software revenue was $6.6 billion, with gross margin 93% and operating margin approximately 76%.
  • Free cash flow in the quarter was $6.4 billion and represented 43% of revenue.
  • Inventory was $2 billion, up 6% sequentially, with days of inventory on hand at 69 days.
  • The company paid $2.8 billion of cash dividends and repurchased $4.2 billion in common stock during Q2.

Q&A

  • Ross Clark Seymore, Deutsche Bank, asked about the drivers for 2026 growth. Hock E. Tan responded, "what we are seeing and what we have quite a bit of visibility increasingly is increased deployment of XPUs next year, much more than we originally thought and hand-in-hand with it, of course, more and more networking."
  • Harlan L. Sur, JPMorgan, queried about sustaining 60% year-over-year AI growth. Tan replied, "over 6 months ago, we gave you guys a point, a year, 2027... we're providing you some level of guidance visibility what we are seeing, how the trajectory of '26 might look like."
  • Benjamin Alexander Reitzes, Melius Research, pressed on AI networking strength and Tomahawk. Tan stated, "the increased density in scale up is 5 to 10x more than in scale out. And that's the part that kind of pleasantly surprised us and which is why this past quarter, Q2, the AI networking portion continues at about 40%."
  • Stacy Aaron Rasgon, Bernstein, sought clarification on AI revenue trajectory. Tan answered, "the rate we are seeing now so far in fiscal '25 and will presumably continue, we don't see any reason why it doesn't given lead time visibility in '25."
  • Joshua Louis Buchalter, TD Cowen, asked about gross margins. Spears explained, "the majority of it quarter-over-quarter, the 130 basis point decline is being driven by more XPUs."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts frequently pressed for details on the sustainability of AI growth and product mix, showing a slightly positive yet probing tone, particularly on future AI revenue and networking trends.
  • Management's tone was confident in prepared remarks, citing "excellent operating leverage" and "momentum," but became more cautious and factual during Q&A, especially when discussing long-term projections and export controls.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's sentiment retained confidence but showed increased willingness to provide visibility into 2026 AI growth while maintaining caution on regulatory and market uncertainties.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 guidance for AI semiconductor revenue increased from $4.4 billion in Q1 to $5.1 billion for Q3.
  • Growth rates in AI revenue and networking outpaced initial expectations, while non-AI semiconductor revenue remained flat, consistent with prior commentary.
  • Management increased transparency regarding 2026 AI revenue prospects, compared to Q1 where focus remained on 2025 and expanding customer engagements.
  • Analysts’ questions in both quarters centered on AI trajectory, customer ramp-ups, and networking mix, but Q2 showed deeper probing into inference-driven demand and product transitions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted that "non-AI semiconductor revenue is close to the bottom, has been relatively slow to recover."
  • Spears highlighted that free cash flow as a percentage of revenue "continues to be impacted by increased interest expense from debt related to the VMware acquisition and increased cash taxes."
  • Tan acknowledged uncertainty around export controls, stating, "rules are changing quite dramatically as trade -- bilateral trade agreements continue to be negotiated in a very, very dynamic environment."

Final Takeaway

Broadcom management emphasized organic revenue growth driven by AI semiconductor and infrastructure software momentum, highlighted by the launch of Tomahawk 6 and robust AI networking demand. The company projects continued strength in AI, forecasting $5.1 billion in Q3 AI semiconductor revenue and expects this growth rate to persist into 2026. Management remains focused on disciplined capital allocation, with ongoing debt reduction and share repurchases, while acknowledging headwinds in non-AI segments and regulatory uncertainties that may affect future performance.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Braze outlines $702M–$706M FY2026 revenue target as OfferFit acquisition accelerates AI-driven growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Braze, Inc. (BRZE) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO William Magnuson reported strong first quarter results, highlighting "$162.1 million of revenue, up nearly 20% year-over-year," and noted efficiency improvements with "over 900 basis points year-over-year" increase in non-GAAP operating margin, along with "our fourth straight quarter of non-GAAP net income profitability, achieving over $7 million of net income and nearly $23 million of free cash flow in the quarter." Magnuson emphasized ongoing momentum despite a "noisy and uneven" environment, with robust bookings and customer wins across industries and geographies.

  • Magnuson announced the completed acquisition of OfferFit, stating that "we successfully closed the acquisition of OfferFit, a leading AI decisioning company that leverages proprietary reinforcement learning to enable brands to deliver highly relevant and personalized customer engagement at scale." He described OfferFit's integration as a catalyst for increasing deal sizes, strengthening Braze's competitive differentiation, and expanding AI-driven optimization capabilities.

  • The CEO introduced Ed McDonnell as the incoming Chief Revenue Officer, citing his experience as former CRO at Salesforce Marketing Cloud and Asana, and expressed confidence in the newly rounded leadership team.

  • CFO Isabelle Winkles stated, "we reported a strong first quarter with revenue increasing 20% year-over-year to $162 million," and detailed that "subscription revenue remains the primary component of our total top line, contributing 96% of our first quarter revenue." Winkles highlighted customer growth, margin expansion, and a healthy contract renewal environment, adding, "Non-GAAP net income attributable to Braze shareholders in the quarter was $7 million or $0.07 per share compared to a loss of $6 million or a loss of $0.05 per share in the prior year quarter."

Outlook

  • Winkles guided for Q2 revenue in the range of "$171 million to $172 million, which represents a year-over-year growth rate of approximately 18% at the midpoint." Q2 non-GAAP operating income is expected between "$0.5 million to $1.5 million" and non-GAAP net income per share between "$0.02 to $0.03 per share based on approximately 113 million weighted average diluted shares outstanding during the period."

  • For fiscal year 2026, revenue guidance is set at "$702 million to $706 million which represents a year-over-year growth rate of approximately 19% at the midpoint." OfferFit is expected to contribute "approximately 2 percentage points to year-over-year revenue growth for the full fiscal year, which equates to approximately $11 million to $12 million."

  • Non-GAAP operating income for FY2026 is projected between "$5.5 million to $9.5 million" and "net income per share is expected to be $0.15 to $0.18 per share based on a full year weighted average diluted share count of approximately 115 million shares."

Financial Results

  • Revenue for the quarter was reported at "$162.1 million," with non-GAAP gross profit at "$112 million, representing a non-GAAP gross margin of 69.3%." Total remaining performance obligation reached "$829.3 million, up 26% year-over-year and up 5% sequentially."

  • Cash provided by operations was "$24 million," and free cash flow was "$23 million" for the quarter. The company ended with "approximately $540 million in cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash and marketable securities."

  • Large customers ($500,000+ ARR) grew to 262, representing a 24% year-over-year increase, and contributed 62% to total ARR.

Q&A

  • Gabriela Borges, Goldman Sachs, questioned when company-specific dynamics would offset macro headwinds and which metrics to track. Winkles responded, "I don't love the CRPO number as a leading indicator. I would say, really look to revenue as the number that we're looking for...to really show the indication that the macro has stabilized for us over the longer term." Magnuson emphasized that "competitively, we feel really good about the results...We saw continued great momentum out of Q4 into Q1."

  • Brent Bracelin, Piper Sandler, asked about backlog linearity and OfferFit's early impact. Winkles stated, "There's nothing special about the linearity that we achieved in the quarter...we were just generally pleased with the overall performance of that in the quarter." Magnuson noted strong customer interest in OfferFit, shifting go-to-market priorities, and plans to scale quickly through Braze's customer base.

  • Pinjalim Bora, JPMorgan, inquired about pricing changes and EBIT impact from OfferFit. Magnuson said, "we launched our new pricing and packaging...and it includes a massive relaxation on data point limits." Winkles clarified, "if you go back to when we announced Q4 and gave guidance for the year, I did indicate that on an organic basis, we expected to add about 400 basis points to non-GAAP operating income. That obviously has come down...by about 300 basis points. So a good portion of that is the impact of OfferFit specifically."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for clarity on revenue acceleration, OfferFit integration, and pricing strategy, reflecting a neutral tone with mild skepticism regarding macro uncertainties and churn trends.

  • Management maintained a confident stance, frequently expressing optimism about competitive strength and integration progress, with Magnuson stating "we are confident that the legacy replacement cycle and vendor consolidation trends will persist, presenting Braze with opportunities to increase market share..."

  • Compared to last quarter, management's tone remains confident but more focused on execution and risk management, while analysts continue to probe for stabilization signals and evidence of accelerating growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue increased to $162.1 million this quarter from $160.4 million last quarter. Customer count rose by 46 sequentially.

  • Non-GAAP net income declined to $7 million from $12 million in the previous quarter, while non-GAAP operating income dropped to $3 million from $8 million.

  • Dollar-based net retention decreased to 109% from 111% last quarter, reflecting some ongoing churn.

  • Full-year revenue guidance was raised to $702–$706 million (from $686–$691 million previously) after factoring in OfferFit, while net income guidance was reduced, reflecting integration and investment costs.

  • Strategic focus shifted toward rapid OfferFit integration, pricing model evolution, and continued churn management.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged elevated churn levels due to seasonality and enterprise renewals but forecasted improvement through the year, citing "the cumulative effects of the last, call it, 6 quarters of very focused preventative efforts around churn."

  • Macro environment described as "noisy and uneven," with attention to global trade concerns, tariff uncertainty, and regional weaknesses, particularly in APAC.

  • Analysts highlighted uncertainty regarding net retention stabilization and the pace of revenue growth recovery, prompting management to direct focus toward revenue as the primary indicator.

Final Takeaway

Braze delivered a strong start to fiscal 2026, driven by nearly 20% revenue growth, robust customer expansion, and continued profitability. The completed OfferFit acquisition is positioned to accelerate AI-driven product differentiation and revenue opportunities, while a new CRO appointment strengthens the leadership bench. Management raised full-year revenue guidance to $702–$706 million, adjusting income expectations to account for integration costs, and signaled confidence in further market share gains, strategic pricing shifts, and ongoing operational efficiency amid a dynamic macro environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Petco reiterates 2025 outlook with EBITDA target of $375M–$390M as transformation efforts advance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Petco Health and Wellness Company, Inc. (WOOF) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Joel D. Anderson highlighted a multi-phased approach to reinventing the Petco brand and restoring retail fundamentals, stating, "Our first quarter performance reflects these ongoing efforts as we delivered top line results in line with expectations, but most importantly, over delivered on our profitability goals." Anderson emphasized the completion of Phase 1 and the transition to Phase 2, which focuses on implementing work streams to improve retail fundamentals and financial performance. He described actions such as product assortment optimization, SKU rationalization, and enhancing shelf capacity, noting, "As we complete the dog food reset, you will see our shelves move about 6 inches higher... increases shelf capacity by more than 10%."
  • The CEO stressed the importance of operational improvements, such as cost discipline and store simplification, and spotlighted services as "the fastest-growing area of the pet category" and a foundation for competitive differentiation.
  • CFO Sabrina Louise Simmons stated, "We entered the year with the #1 financial priority being to restore the health of our economic model through 3 key pillars, gross margin expansion, SG&A leverage and ROIC improvement." Simmons reported a gross margin rate of 38.2% for the quarter, expanded by over 30 basis points, and highlighted expense leverage of more than 180 basis points.

Outlook

  • The company reiterated its full-year 2025 outlook, expecting overall net sales to be down low single digits from the previous year, including the impact of 20 to 30 net store closures. Adjusted EBITDA is projected between $375 million and $390 million.
  • For the second quarter, net sales are expected to be down low single digits versus the prior year, with adjusted EBITDA between $92 million and $94 million. Simmons stated, "Assuming tariffs remain at today's current level and no higher, we believe we can still deliver on the outlook we provided at the start of the year."
  • The company expects depreciation of approximately $200 million, net interest expense of $130 million, and capital expenditures of $125 million to $130 million, focusing on return on invested capital.

Financial Results

  • Comparable sales declined 1.3%, and net sales fell 2.3%, attributed to store closures. Gross margin rate expanded to 38.2%.
  • Operating profit was $16.4 million, an increase of $33 million compared to last year. Adjusted EBITDA rose to $89.4 million, expanding 105 basis points to 6% of sales.
  • Cash balance at quarter end was $139 million, an improvement of $48 million year-over-year. Inventory was managed 5% below last year. Free cash flow was negative $44 million, primarily due to incentive payouts.

Q&A

  • Sachin Verma, UBS, asked about pet adoption trends and their impact on the outlook. Anderson responded that trends are stable and reiterated, "2025 is really a self-help story for Petco. So we're not relying on any tailwinds from the category like pet adoption increase."
  • Verma followed up on permanent cost reduction. Simmons explained, "We're really working on a change in mindset... Our goal is not just about cost cutting. Our goal is to leverage SG&A."
  • Simeon Gutman, Morgan Stanley, asked about the timing of growth initiatives. Anderson replied, "From your guy's perspective on the outside, I would say we haven't even started, you haven't seen it. But... inside we're already beginning to identify several levers of growth."
  • Steven Zaccone, Citi, inquired about gross margin performance and expectations. Simmons stated, "We actually... would start modestly initially on this journey of expanding our margins. So pleased where they came out. AUC was a powerful lever this quarter."
  • Kaumil Gajrawala, Jefferies, asked about the consumer environment and category mix. Anderson said, "Services has continued to lead our business... Grooming is something that probably is more needs-based than, say, supplies."
  • Kendall Toscano, BofA, asked about the slower growth in services. Simmons cited the Vital Care membership business relaunch as a temporary drag, adding, "Our core service business without that is actually quite healthy."
  • Oliver Wintermantel, Evercore, questioned the leverage ratio goal. Simmons emphasized, "The first step forward is the focus on profitability... as we increase profit that will fairly bring the ratio back into a better position."
  • Peter Benedict, Baird, asked about inflation and pricing. Anderson said, "We haven't seen any strong spikes in inflation year-to-date."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral to slightly cautious tone, probing for details on growth timing, cost reductions, margin expansion, and category performance, with several questions focused on ongoing transformation execution and the pace of returning to growth.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, stressing discipline and the multi-phased transformation, with frequent references to "profitable growth," "cost discipline," and the "right strategy." Quotes such as "I'm confident in the detailed multi-phased approach currently in place" reflected this.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst questions were more focused on timing for growth and the impact of operational changes, while management continued to emphasize foundational improvements and margin expansion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company maintained guidance for full-year net sales and adjusted EBITDA, reiterating the same EBITDA target of $375 million to $390 million.
  • Strategic focus continues to shift from cost controls and operational discipline (Phase 1) to execution of productivity and assortment changes (Phase 2), with growth initiatives (Phase 3) set for later in 2025 and into 2026.
  • Analysts in both quarters emphasized timing and sustainability of improvements, but the current quarter saw more questions on the pace of in-store resets and the impact of service business adjustments.
  • Management tone remained confident, with increased specificity around operational improvements and a greater emphasis on recent execution and early signs of productivity gains.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariff uncertainty remains a challenge, with management noting, "Our cross-functional teams... have mobilized together to identify various contingency plans and mitigation strategies."
  • The company cited a "more uncertain macro environment" and the need for "cost discipline" across operations.
  • Analysts expressed concerns about the timing and impact of service business adjustments, category performance, and maintaining customer experience during cost reductions.

Final Takeaway

Petco’s leadership reaffirmed its commitment to a disciplined, multi-phased transformation with a focus on restoring retail fundamentals, expanding gross margins, and leveraging SG&A. The company maintained its full-year outlook despite ongoing tariff headwinds, highlighted operational improvements in services and product assortment, and signaled that foundational changes are paving the way for future growth, with new loyalty and marketing initiatives expected to emerge in 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Torrid targets 180 store closures in 2025 while digital sales approach 70% mix
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Torrid Holdings Inc. (CURV) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Lisa M. Harper, CEO, reported that sub-brands Festi, Belle Isle, Nightfall, and Retro Chic "are overachieving our expectations from 2x to 6x what we had originally planned." She stated these sub-brands are designed to attract new and younger customers, reactivating lapsed customers and creating a halo effect for the mainline Torrid offerings. Harper explained, "with a margin structure higher than our core Torrid product, we are doubling down on our efforts to further expand our strategy with planned launches of new sub-brands throughout the year" and plan to increase delivery frequency from 6-8 to 12 times annually, aiming for sub-brands to comprise up to 30% of the portfolio in 2026.
  • Harper announced an accelerated store optimization plan, stating, "we are accelerating our fleet optimization efforts with a plan to now close approximately 60 stores in the first half of this year" and "an opportunity to close up to an additional 120 stores in the back half of this year, bringing the total number of targeted closures for the year to approximately 180." She emphasized, "the projected impact to net sales is expected to be negligible" due to high retention rates from closed stores and a shift toward digital sales.
  • Harper shared that "our online sales demand continues to grow and is approaching 70% of total sales. We expect web demand to reach a low-to-mid 70% penetration in 2026." The digital transformation plan aims for a "demand mix of 75% online and 25% in store."
  • The company has temporarily paused its shoe offerings, which are fully sourced from China, resulting in an expected revenue loss of approximately $40 million to $45 million but a neutral EBITDA impact for 2025.
  • Harper highlighted tariff mitigation, stating, "our current exposure to China-sourced goods will be in the low-single digits for the balance of the year, down from the mid-teens." The net impact of tariffs is expected to be approximately $20 million for the remainder of the year, offset through expense reductions and store optimization.
  • Paula S. Dempsey, CFO, remarked, "we delivered results in line with expectations for both net sales and adjusted EBITDA in Q1. After a slow start to the quarter in February, we saw improving sales momentum as the quarter progressed. Importantly, we began to realize tangible benefits from our store optimization initiative launched last year, which supported a reduction in SG&A and reinforced our focus on profitability and disciplined cost control."

Outlook

  • The company revised its revenue outlook due to the footwear pause, now expecting full-year net sales in the range of $1.030 billion to $1.055 billion. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to range from $95 million to $105 million for the full year, which reflects net tariff impacts and mitigation efforts. Dempsey stated, "We expect to mitigate approximately $20 million of tariff impact through $20 million in expense reductions for the year."
  • For Q2, net sales are expected between $250 million to $265 million and adjusted EBITDA between $18 million and $24 million.
  • Looking ahead to 2026, management projects a benefit of 150 to 250 basis points of EBITDA margin expansion from store closures and channel realignment.

Financial Results

  • Net sales for Q1 were $266 million, as reported by Harper and confirmed by Dempsey, compared to $279.8 million in the prior year.
  • Comparable store sales declined 3.5%, reflecting ongoing pressure in physical retail, while digital sales continued to grow.
  • Gross profit was $101.4 million, with gross margin at 38.1%. SG&A expenses were $70 million, down from $76.5 million last year, and represented 26.3% of sales.
  • Net income was $5.9 million, or $0.06 per share, compared to $12.2 million, or $0.12 per share, in the prior year. Adjusted EBITDA was $27.1 million, representing a 10.2% margin.
  • Cash and cash equivalents ended at $23.7 million, and total liquidity including borrowing capacity was $141 million. Total debt was reduced to $284.5 million.

Q&A

  • Mary, Bank of America, asked about the cadence of newness for the second half. Harper responded there will be a new sub-brand, Lovesick, launching in August and Studio Luxe in September, with monthly deliveries of existing brands in Q4.
  • Savannah Taylor Sommer, Goldman Sachs, inquired about new customer behavior from sub-brands. Ashlee Wheeler responded, "we are seeing really positive movement in the customer file related to these sub-brands, acquiring and reactivating new and younger customers than our average age in our existing file," and about 90% of sub-brand purchasers are adding core Torrid products to their baskets. Harper added, "it is performing substantially higher online than in stores."
  • Katherine Read Delahunt, Morgan Stanley, questioned the Q2 sales guidance and impact of paused footwear. Dempsey explained, "we are pausing right now our shoe business until further notice... that impacts about $45 million in sales for the year kind of spread evenly through the balance of the year."
  • Dylan Douglas Carden, William Blair, asked about promotional strategy and store closure accelerations. Wheeler confirmed, "we are continuing to be, I would say, as promotional as we typically are," and Harper detailed the rationale for the 75:25 online/store mix, emphasizing customer preference and digital efficacy. On negligible sales impact from closures, Harper said, "we have a high confidence level in our ability to offset the small amount that doesn't naturally transfer with new customer acquisition through the digital channel."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, focusing on operational cadence, digital trends, and sub-brand performance, with some concern about the footwear pause and store closures.
  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently using phrases like "we are confident" and elaborating on mitigation strategies. The tone was more assertive and optimistic compared to the previous quarter, especially regarding digital shift and sub-brand momentum.
  • Compared to Q4, analysts' questions were less skeptical and more focused on tactical execution, while management showed increased confidence in strategic initiatives and digital transformation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw an aggressive acceleration in store closures, with the target rising from 40-50 in the previous quarter to approximately 180 for the year.
  • Management’s tone has shifted towards greater confidence in digital and sub-brand strategies, with more explicit guidance on digital penetration and profitability gains from optimization.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from macro uncertainties and inventory management in Q4 to digital trends, customer acquisition, and execution of sub-brand launches in Q1.
  • Key metrics such as net sales, EBITDA, and margin rates were discussed with a stronger emphasis on cost control and digital migration.
  • There was a more detailed articulation of tariff mitigation and the direct impact of pausing the footwear business.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing consumer price sensitivity and macro volatility impacting conversion rates.
  • There is an expected $20 million net tariff impact for the remainder of the year, with mitigation planned through expense reductions and project reprioritization.
  • The shoe category pause, due to 100% China sourcing, will result in a $40 million to $45 million revenue loss and remains under evaluation for future profitability alignment.
  • Management is focused on offsetting sales losses from store closures through digital investment and customer retention, noting a 60% customer retention rate from closed stores.

Final Takeaway

Torrid Holdings Inc. executed on its strategy of accelerated store fleet optimization, digital expansion, and sub-brand growth in Q1 2025. The company plans to close up to 180 stores this year and expects digital sales to reach a low-to-mid 70% penetration in 2026. Sub-brand momentum continues to outperform expectations, attracting younger and new customers with higher margins. The pause in the shoe category will impact revenue this year but is accompanied by actions to maintain profitability. Management remains confident in offsetting tariff headwinds and sales migration through disciplined expense management, targeted marketing, and a robust digital-first approach, establishing a strong foundation for sustainable, profitable growth.
